meta.concept: Valence Bond Theory, Hybridization, Resonance, and Orbital Overlap

layer_1_hard_formulas.used_for: Calculating the hybridization number (H) of the central atom.

layer_1_hard_formulas.formula: Bond Order (Resonance) = Total number of bonds in all structures / Total number of resonating structures
layer_1_hard_formulas.used_for: Determining fractional bond orders in species like CO3^2-, PO4^3-, or benzene.
Evaluating the most stable resonating structure among multiple possibilities.
Relating hybridization (sp, sp2, sp3) to electronegativity and bond length.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Overlap Criteria: Sigma (σ) bonds are formed by head-on overlap; Pi (π) bonds are formed by lateral/sideways overlap.
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Distinguishing between primary framework bonds and multiple bonds.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Hybridization Constraint: Only atomic orbitals of similar energy belonging to the same atom/ion can undergo hybridization.
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Identifying valid hybrid orbital sets (e.g., 2s and 2p).

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Symmetry of Hybrids: Hybrid orbitals are oriented in space to minimize electron-pair repulsion (VSEPR-aligned orientations).
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Predicting the spatial arrangement of σ-bonds.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Resonance Stability: Structures with more covalent bonds, complete octets, and minimal formal charge separation are more stable.
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Comparing the contribution of various canonical forms to the resonance hybrid.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Orbital Conservation: The number of hybrid orbitals produced is always equal to the number of atomic orbitals mixed.
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Determining the total capacity for bonds and lone pairs on a central atom.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Pi Bond Exclusion: Hybrid orbitals are used for σ-bonds and lone pairs only; π-bonds are formed by unhybridized p-orbitals.
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Calculating the remaining p-orbitals for multiple bonding in sp or sp2 systems.

layer_3_derived_consequences.consequence: As the value of H increases from 2 to 4, the bond angle decreases (180° to 120° to 109.5°).
Molecules with sp hybridization (H=2) are always linear in their σ-bond framework.
In the resonance hybrid of CO3^2-, all three C-O bond lengths are identical and intermediate between single and double bonds.
A higher formal charge on an atom in a specific canonical structure indicates a less significant contribution to the actual hybrid.
Increasing s-character (sp > sp2 > sp3) leads to shorter bond lengths and stronger bonds.
An atom with sp hybridization is more electronegative than the same atom with sp3 hybridization.
A σ-bond is stronger than a π-bond because head-on overlap is more effective than lateral overlap.
Rotation is restricted about a π-bond but free about a σ-bond due to the nature of orbital overlap.
If an atom is sp2 hybridized, it must have one unhybridized p-orbital available to potentially form one π-bond.
The hybrid orbitals are always equivalent in energy and shape, regardless of the parent atomic orbital types.
Resonance hybrid energy is always lower than the energy of any single canonical structure (Resonance Energy stabilization).
Lone pairs are accommodated in hybrid orbitals, occupying the same spatial regions as σ-bonds.
d-orbitals are involved in hybridization only when the central atom is from the 3rd period or higher (e.g., sp3d, sp3d2).
For benzene, the bond order of 1.5 implies that every C-C bond is exactly the same strength.
